parts.content: Discuss the role of sports massage in injury prevention and recovery, and evaluate its effectiveness as part of a rehabilitation program.
parts.markscheme:
### Role of sports massage in injury prevention and recovery

- Improves circulation, reducing muscle tension and preventing scar tissue formation, aiding flexibility <marks>4 marks</marks>

### Role in injury prevention and recovery

- Explanation of how sports massage can prevent overuse injuries and aid in recovery after intense training or injury <marks>3 marks</marks>

### Evaluation of effectiveness and limitations

- Benefits (relaxation, flexibility) vs. limitations (cost, temporary effects) <marks>2 marks</marks>
- Depth and quality of explanation <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Explain how strength training can be used as both a preventive measure and a rehabilitative intervention for sports injuries.
parts.markscheme:
### Strength training as injury prevention <marks>4 marks</marks>

- Strengthening muscles around joints can improve joint stability and reduce risk of injury
- Correcting muscle imbalances (e.g., strong quadriceps and weak hamstrings) can improve biomechanics and reduce strain on joints
- Improving core strength can enhance posture and reduce risk of lower back injuries
- Increasing overall muscle strength can better prepare the body for the demands of sports

### Strength training in rehabilitation <marks>3 marks</marks>

- Gradual and controlled loading of injured muscles/joints can aid in recovery
$$
\text{Progressively increasing resistance and range of motion}
$$
- Targeted exercises can help regain strength and flexibility in affected areas
- Strengthening surrounding muscles can provide support and stability to injured areas

### Risks and considerations <marks>2 marks</marks>

- Proper technique is crucial to avoid further injury or compensation patterns
- Programs should be individualized based on injury type, severity, and athlete's needs
- Improper loading or progression can delay recovery or cause setbacks
<note>Maximum 2 marks if risks and considerations are not addressed</note>

<examTip>Provide specific examples of exercises or techniques where relevant</examTip>

parts.content: Describe the role of functional training in sports injury rehabilitation and critically assess its benefits for an athlete’s return to performance.
parts.markscheme:
### Description of functional training
- Functional training involves exercises that mimic sports-specific movements and activities of daily living 
- It aims to improve overall functional performance and reduce the risk of re-injury by training movement patterns relevant to the athlete's sport or activity  <marks>1 mark</mark>

Examples: agility drills, plyometrics, balance exercises, resistance training with multi-planar movements

### Benefits for recovery and return to sport <marks>3 marks</marks>
- Functional training prepares athletes for the dynamic, multi-planar movements required in their sport 
- It helps bridge the gap between traditional rehabilitation exercises and the demands of competitive sports
- Functional training can improve neuromuscular control, proprioception, and sport-specific skills necessary for a successful return to performance

### Critical assessment of effectiveness
- Functional training can be highly effective when properly designed and implemented with a well-planned, progressive approach <marks>1 marks</marks>
- <commonMistake>Limitations include the need for careful exercise selection and progression to accurately mimic sport-specific skills and demands</commonMistake>
- <examTip>Emphasize the importance of an individualized, sport-specific functional training program for optimal effectiveness</examTip>

<note>Depth and quality of explanation will be assessed based on the overall response</note>

parts.content: Assess the impact of nutrition and hydration on injury recovery and rehabilitation
parts.markscheme:

- Importance of protein for muscle repair 
- Importance of carbohydrates for energy

### Importance of hydration

- Explanation of hydration's role in muscle function, energy levels, and efficient recovery <marks>1 marks</marks>

questions.specification:
A sports medicine research team conducted a 6-week study comparing recovery outcomes in athletes with moderate-grade ligament sprains. Two groups were monitored:
- Group A (NSAID Group): Received non-steroidal anti-inflammatory drugs and standard care (compression, ice, elevation).

- Group B (Control Group): Received only standard care without NSAIDs.

Researchers recorded pain scores (0 = no pain, 10 = severe pain) and functional recovery scores (0–100 scale) weekly. The aim was to evaluate the impact of NSAID use on both subjective pain and objective function.

Figure 1: Pain Score Over Time

![](/api/r2/quiz-image?key=_F0Nk93W-5BE7Z2jYKzyf)

Figure 2: Functional Recovery Over Time

![](/api/r2/quiz-image?key=6svLn03uob7QLsRZChe6y)

labeledSubSubTopics.path: B.3 Injury > B.3.2 Interventions related to injury > B.3.2.2 Initial treatment methods and therapeutic modalities for injury recovery
labeledSubSubTopics.title: B.3.2.2 Initial treatment methods and therapeutic modalities for injury recovery
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: The question focuses on injury recovery interventions: standard acute care (compression, ice, elevation), NSAID use, inflammation/pain management, benefits/drawbacks, and return-to-play decisions using recovery data trends.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: B.1 Generating movement in the body > B.1.2 Structure and function of connective tissues and joints > B.1.2.1 Connective tissues and joint structures supporting movement
labeledSubSubTopics.title: B.1.2.1 Connective tissues and joint structures supporting movement
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Context is a moderate-grade ligament sprain (connective tissue/joint structures), and parts ask about early-phase ligament injury processes (inflammation and pain), which relate to connective tissue injury context.

parts.content: Refer to Figure 1 and identify the week at which the largest difference in pain score is observed between Group A and Group B.
Refer to figure 1 to describe the overall trend in pain scores for both groups from Week 1 to Week 6.
parts.markscheme:
- Both groups show a consistent decrease in pain over the 6-week period.

- Group A (NSAID) has a sharper initial decline, particularly between Weeks 1 and 3.

- Group B shows slower initial improvement but closes the gap by Week 6. <marks>2 marks</marks>


<Note> Award <marks>1 mark</marks> for identifying the general downward trend and <marks>1 mark</marks> for correctly comparing the rate of change or crossover in trends.

parts.content: Using Figure 2, at what week do the functional recovery scores of both groups become equal?
Refer to Figure 2 and compare the rates of improvement in functional recovery for Group A and Group B between Weeks 1 and 4.
parts.markscheme:
- Group A shows a faster initial improvement in functional recovery from Week 1 to Week 3. <marks>1 mark</marks>

- Group B improves more gradually but catches up by Week 5. <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Explain how NSAIDs alleviate pain in the initial phases of ligament injury.
parts.markscheme:
- NSAIDs reduce inflammation by inhibiting cyclooxygenase (COX) enzymes. <marks>1 mark</marks>

- This lowers prostaglandin production, which reduces swelling and pain sensation. <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: State one potential benefit and one potential drawback of using NSAIDs for soft tissue recovery in athletes.
parts.markscheme:
- Potential benefit: Faster pain relief allows earlier mobility or participation in rehabilitation. <marks>1 mark</marks>

- Potential drawback: May suppress inflammation needed for tissue regeneration, possibly delaying long-term healing. <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Explain the use of NSAIDs in return-to-play protocols for athletes, using data trends from both figures and linking to inflammation management and performance readiness.
parts.markscheme:
- NSAID use may be useful for short-term pain control and improving early rehabilitation adherence. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Long-term use may interfere with collagen remodeling or increase risk of re-injury. <marks>1 mark</marks>

- Return-to-play decisions should consider both subjective symptoms (pain scores) and objective function (functional scores). <marks>1 mark</marks>

- Athletes in Group A reported faster symptom relief, but long-term outcomes were similar. <marks>1 mark</marks>

questions.specification: Which statement best explains why concussion symptoms may not improve at a constant rate?

labeledSubSubTopics.path: B.3 Injury > B.3.2 Interventions related to injury > B.3.2.3 Staged recovery process for concussion injuries
labeledSubSubTopics.title: B.3.2.3 Staged recovery process for concussion injuries
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: The question asks why concussion recovery is not linear, aligning with understanding concussion recovery progression where symptoms can fluctuate across stages.

options.content: Symptoms may fluctuate and improve irregularly.
options.markscheme: Correct. Concussion recovery is often non-linear because symptoms can flare up or subside unexpectedly as the brain heals and the individual returns to cognitive and physical activities.

options.content: The brain heals in a predictable timeline.
options.markscheme: Incorrect. The recovery timeline for a concussion is highly individual and varies significantly between patients; it does not follow a fixed, predictable schedule.

options.content: Recovery depends largely on physical training levels.
options.markscheme: Incorrect. While physical fitness may influence general health, concussion recovery depends on many factors including symptom management, cognitive/physical load, age, and previous injury history.

options.content: Concussion recovery is often similar across athletes.
options.markscheme: Incorrect. Recovery varies between individuals and between incidents; even the same athlete may experience different recovery paths for different concussions.

parts.content: Describe the R.I.C.E. method for treating acute injuries and evaluate its effectiveness.
parts.markscheme:
- **Identify** Rest: Avoiding further injury by stopping activity <marks>A1</marks>
- **Identify** Ice: Applying cold to reduce inflammation and slow blood flow <marks>A1</marks>
- **Identify** Compression: Using bandages to limit swelling and provide joint support <marks>A1</marks>
- **Identify** Elevation: Raising the injured area above heart level to promote fluid drainage and reduce swelling <marks>A1</marks>
- **Evaluate** the method as providing essential immediate treatment for acute injuries to reduce pain and further damage <marks>R1</marks>
- **Evaluate** the method as being insufficient for complete recovery, as rehabilitation exercises are often required for long-term restoration of function <marks>R1</marks>

4 marks total

<note>Award up to 2 marks for description and up to 2 marks for evaluation. Max 4 marks total.</note>

parts.content: Discuss the role of rehabilitation exercises in the recovery process from a musculoskeletal injury.
parts.markscheme:
- **Discuss** the restoration of range of motion and flexibility through targeted stretching and mobility work <marks>A1</marks>
- **Discuss** the rebuilding of muscular strength and endurance to support the injured joint and prevent atrophy <marks>A1</marks>
- **Discuss** the improvement of proprioception and balance to restore neuromuscular control <marks>A1</marks>
- **Discuss** the gradual increase in functional movements to prepare the athlete for sport-specific demands <marks>A1</marks>

4 marks total

<note>Exercises should be tailored to the specific injury and individual needs.</note>

parts.content: Explain the importance of proprioceptive training in injury prevention and rehabilitation, and critically assess its role in reducing the risk of reinjury.
parts.markscheme:
- **Define** proprioception as the body's ability to sense its position and movement in space <marks>A1</marks>
- **Explain** that training involves exercises challenging balance and coordination (e.g., balance boards, unstable surfaces) <marks>A1</marks>
- **Explain** how these exercises stimulate proprioceptors in muscles and joints to enhance neuromuscular control <marks>A1</marks>
- **Explain** that improved proprioception allows quicker reactions to external forces, enhancing joint stability <marks>A1</marks>
- **Explain** its role in rehabilitation for regaining functional movement patterns after injuries like ACL tears or ankle sprains <marks>A1</marks>
- **Critically assess** that while effective, it requires regular practice and individualized programming to be successful <marks>R1</marks>
- **Critically assess** the limitation that effectiveness depends on athlete compliance and integration with strength/flexibility training <marks>R1</marks>

6 marks total

<note>Award up to 4 marks for explanation and up to 2 marks for critical assessment. Max 6 marks total.</note>

parts.content: Evaluate the use of therapeutic modalities such as heat and cold therapy in the treatment of sports injuries.
parts.markscheme:
- **Evaluate** cold therapy (cryotherapy): Decreases blood flow, reduces inflammation, and manages pain; most appropriate for acute stages of injury <marks>R1</marks>
- **Evaluate** heat therapy (thermotherapy): Increases blood flow, reduces stiffness, and promotes muscle relaxation; better suited for chronic conditions or later stages of recovery <marks>R1</marks>
- **Identify** appropriate application scenarios: Cold for immediate swelling post-injury; heat for muscle spasms or pre-exercise warm-up of chronic injuries <marks>A1</marks>
- **Discuss** benefits such as cold reducing secondary hypoxic injury and heat increasing tissue extensibility <marks>A1</marks>
- **Evaluate** limitations: Cold can cause tissue damage if applied too long; heat can worsen swelling if applied too early to an acute injury <marks>R1</marks>
- **State** contraindications such as impaired circulation or open wounds <marks>A1</marks>

6 marks total

parts.content: Analyze the role of psychological interventions in the recovery and rehabilitation of injured athletes.
parts.markscheme:
- **Analyze** goal setting: Establishing realistic, measurable milestones to maintain focus and track rehabilitation progress <marks>A1</marks>
- **Analyze** visualization/mental imagery: Rehearsing successful movements and recovery outcomes to maintain motor patterns and confidence <marks>A1</marks>
- **Analyze** relaxation techniques: Using deep breathing or progressive muscle relaxation to manage the stress/anxiety of being injured <marks>A1</marks>
- **Analyze** self-talk: Employing positive affirmations to counter negative emotions and maintain motivation during setbacks <marks>A1</marks>
- **Explain** how these interventions improve adherence to demanding rehabilitation programs <marks>R1</marks>
- **Explain** how psychological support enhances coping strategies for dealing with frustrations or fear of reinjury <marks>R1</marks>

4 marks total

<note>Award max 4 marks total.</note>

questions.specification: Which scenario best demonstrates absorbing/attenuating ground reaction force by increasing deceleration time to prevent injury?

labeledSubSubTopics.path: B.3 Injury > B.3.2 Interventions related to injury > B.3.2.1 Strategies to minimize risk and maximize the body's ability to absorb forces
labeledSubSubTopics.title: B.3.2.1 Strategies to minimize risk and maximize the body's ability to absorb forces
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: The question tests the injury-prevention principle of absorbing force (e.g., bending knees on landing) to reduce impact and injury risk, which directly matches strategies to minimize risk and maximize the body's ability to absorb forces.

options.content: A gymnast landing with bent knees to reduce impact
options.markscheme: Correct: landing with increased knee flexion increases time/distance over which momentum is reduced, lowering peak force.

options.content: A gymnast landing with straight (extended) knees on the floor
options.markscheme: Incorrect: reduced joint flexion shortens deceleration time and typically increases peak ground reaction force.

options.content: A footballer wearing a helmet to reduce head injury risk
options.markscheme: Incorrect: protective equipment reduces injury risk mainly via padding/force distribution, not primarily via a landing/technique-based increase in deceleration time.

options.content: A volleyball player rolling after a dive to spread the impact over a longer time and distance
options.markscheme: Incorrect: this can also be considered a force-attenuation technique, but the question focuses on attenuating *ground reaction force* in a landing; option A is the best example in that context.

questions.specification: Which of the following is a method of lowering the risk of injury?

labeledSubSubTopics.path: B.3 Injury > B.3.2 Interventions related to injury > B.3.2.1 Strategies to minimize risk and maximize the body's ability to absorb forces
labeledSubSubTopics.title: B.3.2.1 Strategies to minimize risk and maximize the body's ability to absorb forces
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: The question asks for a method to lower injury risk (e.g., wearing protective equipment), which aligns with strategies to minimize risk and improve the body’s ability to absorb forces.

options.content: Training on uneven surfaces
options.markscheme: Incorrect: this can increase risk if introduced inappropriately and is not a standard general injury-prevention method.

options.content: Wearing protective equipment
options.markscheme: Correct: appropriate protective equipment can reduce the risk of injury by absorbing/redistributing forces and reducing direct impact to vulnerable areas.

options.content: Progressive overload (gradually increasing training load)
options.markscheme: Incorrect: while progressive overload is a sound training principle, increasing load still raises stress on tissues and is not typically the single best method cited for lowering injury risk in this context.

options.content: Static stretching immediately before explosive activity
options.markscheme: Incorrect: static stretching immediately before explosive activity is not generally recommended as an injury-prevention method and may reduce performance; warm-up should be more dynamic/sport-specific.

options.content: Absence of all symptoms and medical clearance
Completing fitness testing only
Self-reported readiness by the athlete
Performing well in training sessions

options.content: Cryotherapy

options.content: Video analysis
options.markscheme: Incorrect: video analysis is primarily used for performance/technique assessment rather than as a therapeutic treatment modality.

options.content: Ultrasound therapy
options.markscheme: Correct: therapeutic ultrasound is a physical modality used in rehabilitation to aid tissue healing and manage pain.

options.content: Goal-setting
options.markscheme: Incorrect: goal-setting is a psychological strategy that can support rehabilitation adherence, but it is not a physical therapeutic modality.

questions.specification: A long-distance runner begins recovery after a stress fracture. Which combination of treatments reflects best practice in SEHS for stress-fracture rehabilitation?

labeledSubSubTopics.path: B.3 Injury > B.3.2 Interventions related to injury > B.3.2.2 Initial treatment methods and therapeutic modalities for injury recovery
labeledSubSubTopics.title: B.3.2.2 Initial treatment methods and therapeutic modalities for injury recovery
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: The question asks for evidence-based treatment combinations during recovery from a stress fracture, focusing on appropriate therapeutic modalities (e.g., anti-inflammatory management, physiotherapy, gradual return/loading) versus inappropriate options.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: B.3 Injury > B.3.1 Causes of injury > B.3.1.2 Differences between acute trauma and cumulative trauma injuries
labeledSubSubTopics.title: B.3.1.2 Differences between acute trauma and cumulative trauma injuries
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: A stress fracture is a cumulative/overuse injury; recognizing it as cumulative trauma provides context for why gradual load progression and rehab are indicated.

options.content: Anti-inflammatory treatment, gradual load progression, and physiotherapy
options.markscheme: Correct: combines symptom management with graded return to loading and rehabilitation support.

options.content: Ice packs and rest, but no graded return-to-run plan
options.markscheme: Partially appropriate for short-term symptom management, but lacks progressive loading/rehabilitation needed for return to sport.

options.content: Anti-inflammatory treatment and immediate full training once pain decreases
options.markscheme: Premature return to full loading increases re-injury risk; should progress load gradually.

options.content: Surgical repair and prolonged complete rest for all stress fractures
options.markscheme: Surgery is not routinely indicated for most stress fractures; complete rest alone is not best-practice rehabilitation.

questions.specification: Why is dynamic stretching recommended as a part of injury prevention?

labeledSubSubTopics.title: B.3.2.1 Strategies to minimize risk and maximize the body's ability to absorb forces
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Dynamic stretching is a warm-up strategy used to reduce injury risk by preparing muscles/joints to absorb forces and increasing range of motion, aligning with injury risk-minimization strategies.

options.content: It improves flexibility by holding muscles in a stretched position for an extended time.
options.markscheme: Incorrect. This describes static stretching rather than dynamic stretching.

options.content: It helps warm up muscles and improves range of motion.
options.markscheme: Correct. Dynamic stretching increases muscle temperature and prepares joints through movement, improving range of motion and reducing injury risk.

options.content: It increases muscle temperature and activates movement patterns similar to the activity.
options.markscheme: Incorrect. This is a true benefit of dynamic stretching, but the question requires the single best answer; option B is the most complete.

options.content: It prevents injury mainly by increasing muscle size and strength before exercise.
options.markscheme: Incorrect. Injury prevention from dynamic stretching is not primarily through acute increases in muscle size/strength.

questions.specification: Which is the primary goal of early injury treatment?

labeledSubSubTopics.title: B.3.2.2 Initial treatment methods and therapeutic modalities for injury recovery

options.content: Reduce swelling and bleeding
options.markscheme: Correct. Early/acute injury management aims to limit bleeding and swelling and minimize secondary tissue damage (e.g., PRICE/POLICE principles).

options.content: Increase swelling to speed healing
options.markscheme: Incorrect. Increasing swelling is not a goal of early injury management and may increase secondary damage and pain.

options.content: Delay return to sport until all inflammation stops
options.markscheme: Incorrect. Early management focuses on protecting the injury and controlling bleeding/swelling; return-to-sport decisions are not the primary early goal.

options.content: Strengthen opposing muscles immediately
options.markscheme: Incorrect. Strengthening may be part of later rehabilitation, not the primary goal in the early/acute phase.

questions.specification: Why do displaced or unstable fractures sometimes require surgical repair?

labeledSubSubTopics.path: B.3 Injury > B.3.2 Interventions related to injury > B.3.2.2 Initial treatment methods and therapeutic modalities for injury recovery
labeledSubSubTopics.title: B.3.2.2 Initial treatment methods and therapeutic modalities for injury recovery
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: The question asks why major fractures may require surgical repair, which falls under treatment/interventions and therapeutic modalities used in injury recovery (e.g., fixation to realign/stabilize bones).

options.content: To restore and maintain correct alignment and stability when casting or splinting is insufficient
options.markscheme: Correct: surgery may be needed to achieve/maintain anatomical reduction and stable fixation when non-surgical immobilization cannot.

options.content: Because the body heals faster without surgery
options.markscheme: Incorrect: surgery is not avoided to speed healing; it is used when stability/alignment or other complications require it.

options.content: Because surgery always reduces recovery time by half
options.markscheme: Incorrect: recovery time varies; surgery does not always halve recovery time.

options.content: Because surgery eliminates the need for rehabilitation
options.markscheme: Incorrect: rehabilitation is typically still required after surgical repair.

questions.specification: How can foam rolling help in the recovery process?

labeledSubSubTopics.path: A.3 Response > A.3.3 Fatigue and recovery (HL) > A.3.3.2 Strategies and techniques for exercise recovery (HL)
labeledSubSubTopics.title: A.3.3.2 Strategies and techniques for exercise recovery (HL)
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Foam rolling is a recovery technique (self-myofascial release) used post-exercise to reduce DOMS and improve flexibility, fitting strategies/techniques for exercise recovery.

options.content: By increasing the rate of blood lactate removal immediately after exercise
options.markscheme: Foam rolling may increase blood flow and range of motion, but it is not a primary method for immediate blood lactate removal compared with active recovery.

options.content: By increasing the risk of new injuries
options.markscheme: Foam rolling is a recovery technique intended to reduce, not increase, the risk of injury when used appropriately.

options.content: By reducing muscle soreness and improving flexibility
options.markscheme: Foam rolling, a form of self-myofascial release, is used to alleviate delayed onset muscle soreness (DOMS) and improve range of motion/flexibility.

options.content: By replacing the need for stretching
options.markscheme: Foam rolling is often used in conjunction with stretching rather than as a replacement for it.
